Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- Plan: RIGHT to clear the column, then UP to move white box up, then LEFT to align with left box so white box overlaps it.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me analyze the current state:
- Player: [24-26, 20-23] (3x4), color N
- White box: [27-31, 20-23] (4x5), color W
- Left box: [28-31, 16-19] (4x4), color c
- Created box: [32-35, 36-39] (4x4), color c
The white box is at [27-31, 20-23] and the left box is at [28-31, 16-19]. They overlap in rows 28-31 but not in columns (white box cols 20-23, left box cols 16-19). They're adjacent but not overlapping.
The player is at [24-26, 20-23]. The LEFT movement didn't work as expected - the player ended up at cols 20-23 instead of cols 16-19. This is because the LEFT was blocked by the left box at cols 16-19.
So the player is at [24-26, 20-23] and the white box is at [27-31, 20-23]. The left box is at [28-31, 16-19].
The white box overlaps with the left box in rows 28-31 but not in columns. The white box is to the RIGHT of the left box.
I need to move the white box to overlap with the left box. To do that, I need to move the player LEFT, which would move the white box LEFT too.
But the player is at cols 20-23 and the left box is at cols 16-19. If I move LEFT, the player would go to cols 16-19, but the left box is there at rows 28-31. The player is at rows 24-26, so there's no overlap - the player can move LEFT.
Wait, but the previous LEFT didn't work. Let me check why. The previous sequence was RIGHT, UP, UP, LEFT. After RIGHT, the player was at [32-34, 20-23]. After UP, UP, the player was at [26-28, 20-23] (or similar). Then LEFT moved the player to [24-26, 20-23]... wait, that doesn't make sense. LEFT should move the player to the left, not up.
Actually, let me re-examine. The player shape is 3x4 (3 rows, 4 cols). After RIGHT, the shape becomes 4x3 (4 rows, 3 cols). After UP, the shape becomes 3x4. After UP again, still 3x4. After LEFT, the shape becomes 4x3.
Hmm, but the player ended up at [24-26, 20-23], which is 3 rows (24-26) and 4 cols (20-23). So the shape is 3x4, not 4x3. This means the LEFT didn't change the shape, which suggests the LEFT was blocked or didn't execute properly.
Wait, actually, looking at the output more carefully:
- Player: rows [24-26], cols [20-23], px=12
- 3 rows x 4 cols = 12 pixels, so shape is 3x4
The LEFT movement didn't change the player's columns (still 20-23). This means the LEFT was blocked.
Why was LEFT blocked? The player is at [24-26, 20-23]. Moving LEFT would put the player at [24-26, 16-19]. But the left box is at [28-31, 16-19]. The player at [24-26, 16-19] doesn't overlap with the left box at [28-31, 16-19] (rows 24-26 vs 28-31, no overlap).
But the white box is at [27-31, 20-23]. If the player moves LEFT, the white box would move LEFT to [27-31, 16-19]. The left box is at [28-31, 16-19]. The white box at [27-31, 16-19] would overlap with the left box at [28-31, 16-19]!
So maybe the LEFT was blocked because the white box would overlap with the left box, and the game prevents that?
Or maybe the LEFT was blocked for a different reason. Let me try LEFT again and see what happens.
Actually, wait. Looking at the image, I can see the player (green) is above the left box (charcoal with blue inside). The white box is to the right of the left box. The player and white box are in the same column.
Let me try moving LEFT again. If the white box overlaps with the left box, maybe that activates the left box!

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at [24-26, 20-23] (3x4), white box at [27-31, 20-23]. Left box at [28-31, 16-19] still c. White box and left box overlap in rows 28-31 but not columns. LEFT was blocked previously - possibly because white box would overlap left box.
Plan: Try LEFT again - if white box overlaps left box, it might activate it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT to move white box into left box
result = action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'G', 'W', 'N'] and n['pixels'] >= 8:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], px={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  color=c, rows [32-35], cols [36-39], px=12
  color=c, rows [63-63], cols [47-63], px=17
